subsection: I.B. LIMITATIONS OF CURRENTLY USED DOSE-VOLUME-BASED TREATMENT PLANNING
citation: [NAV] AAPM Report 166: The use and QA of biologically related models for treatment planning; The Use and QA of Biologically Related Models for Treatment Planning; p. 13; chunk aapm_report_166_biological_models_treatment_planning_qa_c0012

document: AAPM Report 166: The use and QA of biologically related models for treatment planning
section: The Use and QA of Biologically Related Models for Treatment Planning
subsection: I.C. SCOPE AND TERMINOLOGY Historically, dose prescription in RT has been performed using population-based knowledge about behavior of a particular type of tumor or normal tissue. Rapid advances in functional imaging, molecular techniques, predictive assays, and RT delivery technology will sooner or later enable implementing truly-individualized RT in the clinic (Stewart and Li 2007). This task group report (TG-166) will refer to a framework of RT that takes advantage of information about spatial and temporal distribution of relevant patient-specific biological parameters, such as tumor and normal cell radiosensitivity, oxygenation status, proliferation rate, etc., as biologically guided radiation therapy (BGRT). Other investigators have previously used various alternate
